evidence: The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.
Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.
Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.
Ivor engaged substantively with product demo, shared strategic advice unprompted, and immediately grasped Careerspan's edge
Ivor actively searched LinkedIn for Handshake contacts during the call. Offered to have new hire reach out proactively. Said 'let me see who else I know at Handshake'
V confirmed Handshake is the 'actual perfect acquirer' - existing product-market fit with career centers; Handshake has distribution to all colleges
Handshake acquisition path validated by external party (Ivor suggested unprompted); strategic ops channel established

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters - this reframes product priorities, marketing messaging, and technical roadmap

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Employers have selection criteria they cannot legally state. The gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences is a structural feature of hiring markets, not a bug.

source_display: Information Efficiency Has Inverted Recruiter Value
evidence: An Indian recruiter placing US engineers said: 'I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.'

source_display: Response Rate as the Only Metric That Matters
evidence: Logan's insight: use response rates as the convincing proxy — 'our users pay attention to our recommendations'
Recruiters who survive will need access to information that doesn't exist in public profiles: behavioral data, cultural signals, engagement patterns - evolves standstill thesis into competitive moat

Ilse's recollection of a generated Sora video—an auditorium full of candidates that empties to three people after clicking a Careerspan button—is a remarkably clean articulation of the value prop: hiring is too loud, and we make it quiet
Superposition wouldn't bring users. They might not even bring significant revenue. But their logo creates a trust cascade. This is pure signaling economics—the brand IS the product.
V's counter to 'you only have 500 users' was essentially: 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' This inverts the network effects playbook.

Logan's biotech friend disrupted her industry by going directly to morgues instead of through middlemen. Provocative question: Who is Careerspan's 'middleman' that could be bypassed? Alumni associations themselves? Recruiters? LinkedIn?

Decision: Pursue both brand-name partnerships (Superposition) for credibility AND hungry individual recruiters for execution. Brand names provide website legitimacy; individual recruiters drive actual success stories.
Prioritize New York City for high-signal user acquisition. Channels: Harvard Club of NYC, Cornell Club of NYC, AI Circle (via Albert), NYC-specific alumni networks.
NYC focus decision reinforces existing thesis about NYC tech ecosystem; AI Circle via Albert cited as target community
